articles.headline: India Unveils Second Phase of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Targeting Global Leadership
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) announced the second phase of the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) on January 22, 2025.
• This phase focuses on scaling up production capabilities, establishing dedicated green hydrogen corridors, and boosting domestic manufacturing of electrolysers.
• Key targets include achieving 5 million tonnes of annual green hydrogen production capacity by 2030 and significant reduction in green hydrogen production costs.
• The mission aims to position India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization goals.
• New incentives for R&D in fuel cell technology and green hydrogen applications in hard-to-abate sectors like steel and heavy-duty transport are part of the expanded scope.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure
Environmental pollution & degradation
Science & Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Mobilization of resources
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy / The Economic Times
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) launched in 2023.
Target: 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) annual green hydrogen production by 2030.
Estimated investment: ₹8 lakh crore.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE).
SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a key component.
Applications: Fertilizers, refineries, steel, cement, heavy-duty mobility.
mainsPoints: Economic Impact: Job creation, investment attraction, reduced fossil fuel imports (energy security), export potential, industrial growth.
Environmental Impact: Decarbonization of industries, reduction in greenhouse gas emissions, contribution to India's climate targets (NDC).
Strategic Significance: Self-reliance in energy, geopolitical leverage, leadership in green technologies.
Challenges: High initial capital costs, technological hurdles, infrastructure development (storage, transport), water availability for electrolysis.
Government Initiatives: Production Linked Incentives (PLI) for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production, R&D support, demand creation.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating the green hydrogen production process with solar panels and an electrolyser.
articles.trivia: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity, making it a zero-emission fuel.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Autonomy of State Election Commissions in Local Body Polls
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, on January 22, 2025, reaffirmed the independent and autonomous nature of State Election Commissions (SECs) in conducting local body elections.
• The ruling clarified that state governments cannot unduly interfere with the schedule or conduct of Panchayat and Municipal elections.
• The court emphasized the constitutional mandate of SECs under Articles 243K and 243ZA, highlighting their role in ensuring free and fair elections at the grassroots level.
• This judgment reinforces the federal structure and the principles of democratic decentralization enshrined in the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments.
• It called for greater cooperation between state governments and SECs to uphold the integrity of the electoral process.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: State Election Commissions are constitutional bodies responsible for conducting elections to Panchayats and Municipalities.
Articles 243K (Panchayats) and 243ZA (Municipalities) deal with SECs.
The conditions of service and tenure of an SEC cannot be varied to his disadvantage after his appointment.
SEC enjoys the same status as a judge of a High Court.
The 73rd and 74th Amendment Acts gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions and Urban Local Bodies, respectively.
mainsPoints: Significance of SEC's autonomy for ensuring free and fair elections at the grassroots level, strengthening democratic decentralization.
Challenges posed by state interference in SEC's functions and its impact on the credibility of local governance.
Role of judicial intervention in upholding constitutional provisions and safeguarding the independence of statutory bodies.
Implications for federal relations and the balance of power between state governments and autonomous electoral bodies.
Need for electoral reforms at the local level to enhance transparency and accountability.
articles.imageDescription: Scales of justice with a map of India in the background, symbolizing judicial oversight on federal structures.
articles.trivia: The first State Election Commission was established in Madhya Pradesh in 1994.

articles.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizing Deeper Indo-Pacific Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue, held in New Delhi on January 22, 2025, concluded with a joint statement reinforcing commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, counter-terrorism efforts, space technology collaboration, and maritime security.
• Both nations reiterated their resolve to address regional and global challenges, including climate change and cyber security threats.
• India welcomed France's continued engagement in the Indo-Pacific, recognizing its role as a resident power with significant interests.
• New initiatives were announced for joint research and development in critical and emerging technologies, solidifying the strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
articles.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Times of India
prelimsPoints: India and France established a Strategic Partnership in 1998.
France is a key partner for India in defense, nuclear energy, and space.
India's Rafale fighter jets are sourced from France.
Both nations are committed to multilateralism and rules-based international order.
France has significant overseas territories in the Indo-Pacific (e.g., Reunion, New Caledonia).
mainsPoints: Significance of India-France strategic partnership in promoting stability and security in the Indo-Pacific region.
How bilateral cooperation in defense and space contributes to India's strategic autonomy and technological self-reliance.
Role of India-France collaboration in addressing global challenges like climate change, terrorism, and cyber security.
Impact of France's Indo-Pacific strategy on regional dynamics and its alignment with India's Act East Policy.
Challenges and opportunities in expanding economic and cultural ties between the two nations.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed during a joint press conference.
articles.trivia: France was the first P5 country to support India's bid for a permanent seat in the UN Security Council.

articles.headline: RBI's Monetary Policy Committee Maintains Status Quo on Key Rates Amidst Inflationary Pressures
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its review on January 22, 2025, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision was driven by persistent inflationary concerns, particularly in food prices, despite signs of moderating core inflation.
• The MPC reiterated its stance of 'withdrawal of accommodation' to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting growth.
• RBI Governor highlighted the need for careful monitoring of global economic uncertainties and their potential impact on domestic inflation and growth.
• The committee projected India's GDP growth for FY26 to remain robust, while inflation is expected to gradually ease but stay above the 4% target for some time.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary Policy
articles.source: Reserve Bank of India / Business Standard
prelimsPoints: Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has six members: three from RBI and three appointed by the government.
Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation target for RBI is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
The 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance implies reducing liquidity from the system.
The MPC meets at least four times a year.
mainsPoints: Role of monetary policy in balancing inflation control and economic growth in India.
Impact of global economic factors (e.g., crude oil prices, geopolitical tensions) on domestic inflation and RBI's policy decisions.
Challenges in managing food inflation and its disproportionate impact on vulnerable sections of society.
Debate on the effectiveness of monetary policy tools versus fiscal measures in addressing economic challenges.
Future outlook for India's economy given the current monetary policy stance and global uncertainties.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India logo with a graph showing stable interest rates.
articles.trivia: The MPC was established in 2016, moving away from the Governor's sole discretion in setting interest rates.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite EOS-07
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched its latest Earth Observation Satellite, EOS-07, from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre on January 22, 2025.
• The satellite was carried aboard the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV-C58) and placed into a sun-synchronous orbit.
• EOS-07 is equipped with high-resolution optical instruments for advanced imaging, designed to provide data for urban planning, disaster management, and agricultural monitoring.
• This mission further enhances India's capabilities in remote sensing and contributes to national development goals.
• The launch also carried several co-passenger payloads, including student satellites, demonstrating ISRO's commitment to fostering space education.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: EOS series satellites are designed for Earth observation applications.
PSLV is ISRO's third-generation launch vehicle, known for its reliability.
Sun-synchronous orbit allows a satellite to pass over any given point of the Earth's surface at the same local mean solar time.
Applications of EOS include cartography, oceanography, forestry, and disaster management.
Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) in Sriharikota, Andhra Pradesh, is India's primary spaceport.
mainsPoints: Significance of indigenous space technology in achieving self-reliance and national development goals.
Contribution of Earth Observation Satellites to various sectors like agriculture, urban planning, and disaster management.
India's growing role in global space economy and its potential for commercial launches.
Challenges and opportunities in advancing India's space program, including private sector participation and international collaborations.
Ethical considerations and policy frameworks for responsible use of space technology and data.
articles.imageDescription: A PSLV rocket lifting off from the launchpad, with 'ISRO' visible on its side.
articles.trivia: PSLV has successfully launched over 50 missions, including India's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) and Chandrayaan-1.

articles.headline: Ministry of Environment Unveils New Comprehensive Wetlands Conservation Plan for Ramsar Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) launched a new comprehensive 'National Wetlands Conservation and Management Plan' on January 22, 2025.
• The plan focuses on integrated management of India's Ramsar Sites, emphasizing ecological restoration, sustainable use, and community participation.
• It introduces advanced monitoring mechanisms, including remote sensing and AI-powered tools, to track wetland health and combat encroachment.
• Capacity building programs for local communities and forest department officials are central to the plan, promoting responsible wetland stewardship.
• The initiative aims to enhance biodiversity, mitigate climate change impacts, and ensure the ecological services provided by wetlands are preserved.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity.
prelimsPoints: India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands (1971).
Ramsar Sites are wetlands of international importance.
Wetlands provide crucial ecosystem services like water purification, flood control, and biodiversity support.
The Montreux Record is a register of Ramsar sites where changes in ecological character have occurred, are occurring, or are likely to occur.
National Wetland Committee advises the central government on wetland conservation.
mainsPoints: Importance of wetlands for biodiversity conservation, climate change mitigation, and supporting livelihoods.
Challenges faced by wetlands in India, including encroachment, pollution, and unsustainable resource extraction.
Effectiveness of policy frameworks and management plans in achieving wetland conservation goals.
Role of community participation and traditional ecological knowledge in sustainable wetland management.
Linkages between wetland health, water security, and disaster resilience, especially in the context of urban flooding.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds and lush vegetation.
articles.trivia: Chilika Lake in Odisha was designated as India's first Ramsar site in 1981.

articles.headline: Renowned Urdu Poet and Scholar Tabish Mehdi Passes Away at 73
articles.explainer:
• Celebrated Urdu poet, critic, and scholar, Tabish Mehdi, passed away at the age of 73 on January 22, 2025.
• Mehdi was known for his profound contribution to modern Urdu poetry, combining classical aesthetics with contemporary themes.
• His works often explored themes of humanism, social justice, and philosophical introspection, resonating with a wide audience.
• He was also a respected academic, having taught at various universities and contributing significantly to Urdu literary criticism.
• His demise marks a significant loss for the Urdu literary world, leaving behind a rich legacy of poetry and critical thought.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: Tabish Mehdi was a prominent figure in modern Urdu poetry.
Known for blending classical and contemporary styles.
His work often reflected themes of social consciousness and philosophical depth.
Urdu is one of the 22 scheduled languages of India.
Urdu literature has a rich history in India, with notable poets like Ghalib, Iqbal, and Faiz.
articles.imageDescription: A black and white portrait of Tabish Mehdi, deep in thought.
articles.trivia: Urdu developed in the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal Empire, with significant influences from Persian, Arabic, and local Indo-Aryan languages.

articles.headline: India Enhances Coastal Security with Deployment of Advanced Surveillance Systems
articles.explainer:
• India has significantly upgraded its coastal security infrastructure by deploying advanced radar and sensor-based surveillance systems along its coastline on January 22, 2025.
• The new systems are integrated with the existing Coastal Surveillance Network (CSN) and will provide real-time monitoring of maritime activities.
• This move aims to bolster defense against maritime threats, including infiltration, smuggling, and illegal fishing.
• The Indian Coast Guard and Navy will utilize these systems for enhanced maritime domain awareness and quicker response capabilities.
• The initiative is part of a broader strategy to secure India's vast coastline and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) following past security breaches.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: India has a coastline of approximately 7,516 km.
The Indian Coast Guard (ICG) is the primary agency for coastal security in peacetime.
The Indian Navy is responsible for overall maritime security.
The Coastal Security Scheme (CSS) was launched post 26/11 Mumbai attacks.
200 nautical miles from the baseline is India's Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
mainsPoints: Challenges in securing India's vast and porous coastline against various threats (terrorism, smuggling, illegal migration, piracy).
Importance of integrated coastal security architecture involving multiple agencies (Navy, ICG, Marine Police, Customs).
Role of technology, such as advanced surveillance and radar systems, in enhancing maritime domain awareness.
Impact of coastal security on India's blue economy and regional stability.
Measures needed for improving coordination and intelligence sharing among different security agencies.
articles.imageDescription: A radar dish rotating against a coastal backdrop, symbolizing maritime surveillance.
articles.trivia: The Sagar Prahari Bal (SPB) is a special force of the Indian Navy, specifically trained for coastal security.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Reach to Remote and Tribal Areas
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced the expansion of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to cover remote and tribal regions across India on January 22, 2025.
• This expansion aims to bridge the digital health divide and ensure equitable access to digital health services for underserved populations.
• Special focus will be given to creating ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) IDs and digitizing health records in these areas through mobile health units and community health workers.
• The initiative includes training local healthcare providers in using digital tools and improving telemedicine infrastructure.
• This move is crucial for achieving universal health coverage and integrating these populations into the national digital health ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched in September 2021 by the Prime Minister.
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
It is a voluntary opt-in scheme for citizens.
mainsPoints: Significance of digital health initiatives in improving healthcare access and quality, especially in remote areas.
Challenges in implementing digital health solutions in tribal and underserved regions (digital literacy, connectivity, infrastructure).
Potential of ABDM to streamline healthcare delivery, reduce out-of-pocket expenditure, and enhance patient data management.
Role of community health workers (ASHAs) and local governance in promoting digital health adoption.
Ethical considerations around data privacy and security in a national digital health ecosystem.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a smartphone with health app icons, connecting remote areas to healthcare.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) provides health insurance coverage to over 50 crore beneficiaries.

articles.headline: BharatNet Project's Phase III Shows Significant Progress in Rural Broadband Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) released a progress report on January 22, 2025, highlighting significant advancements in Phase III of the BharatNet Project.
• Phase III focuses on upgrading the existing fiber network, extending connectivity to all Gram Panchayats and habitations, and providing last-mile broadband services.
• The report indicated that over 1.5 lakh Gram Panchayats have now been connected with high-speed optical fiber, enhancing digital inclusion in rural India.
• Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models are being extensively utilized to accelerate deployment and ensure sustainable operation of the network.
• This ambitious project aims to bridge the digital divide, facilitate e-governance, and boost rural economic development through ubiquitous broadband access.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: BharatNet Project (formerly National Optical Fibre Network - NOFN) was launched in 2011.
It aims to connect all Gram Panchayats with high-speed broadband.
Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) provides financial support for the project.
Phase III focuses on future-proof network infrastructure and last-mile connectivity.
BBNL (Bharat Broadband Network Limited) is the Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) for BharatNet.
mainsPoints: Significance of rural broadband connectivity for digital inclusion, e-governance, education, and healthcare in India.
Challenges in project implementation, including right-of-way issues, maintenance, and ensuring quality of service in remote areas.
Effectiveness of PPP models in accelerating infrastructure development and promoting efficiency.
Impact of BharatNet on rural economy, entrepreneurship, and empowerment of local communities.
Policy measures needed to ensure optimal utilization of the developed infrastructure and address the digital literacy gap.
articles.imageDescription: A fiber optic cable extending through a rural landscape, connecting villages.
articles.trivia: The Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) was established under the Indian Telegraph Act, 1885, to provide telecom services in rural and remote areas.

articles.headline: India Advocates for Enhanced Climate Finance Commitments at UN Negotiations
articles.explainer:
• India's delegation actively participated in the ongoing UN climate finance negotiations on January 22, 2025, advocating for developed nations to fulfill their commitments.
• India emphasized the need for a clear roadmap and increased financial flows from developed to developing countries for climate mitigation and adaptation.
• The discussions focused on operationalizing the new collective quantified goal (NCQG) on climate finance post-2025, moving beyond the unfulfilled $100 billion target.
• India highlighted the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) in determining finance contributions.
• The talks are crucial in setting the stage for future climate action and ensuring equity in global climate efforts.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
articles.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Times of India
keyTerms: Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
prelimsPoints: UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change) is the primary international treaty on climate change.
Developed countries pledged $100 billion per year in climate finance by 2020, which has not been fully met.
NCQG (New Collective Quantified Goal) is being negotiated for climate finance post-2025.
CBDR-RC is a principle under UNFCCC recognizing different responsibilities based on historical emissions and capabilities.
India is a key voice for developing countries in climate negotiations.
mainsPoints: Importance of climate finance for developing countries to meet their climate goals and adapt to climate change impacts.
Challenges in mobilizing adequate and predictable climate finance, including issues of definition, transparency, and accessibility.
India's stance on climate justice, equity, and the principle of CBDR-RC in global climate negotiations.
Role of international cooperation and multilateral institutions in facilitating climate finance and technology transfer.
Implications of climate finance outcomes for India's energy transition, sustainable development, and global standing.
articles.imageDescription: Delegates from various countries seated around a table during a UN climate negotiation.
articles.trivia: The Green Climate Fund (GCF) is a fund established within the framework of the UNFCCC to assist developing countries in adaptation and mitigation practices.

articles.headline: Indian Scientists Announce Breakthrough in Indigenous Vaccine for Neglected Tropical Disease
articles.explainer:
• Indian researchers at the National Institute of Immunology (NII) announced a significant breakthrough on January 22, 2025, in developing an indigenous vaccine for a neglected tropical disease.
• The vaccine candidate, currently in advanced preclinical trials, has shown promising results in generating robust immune responses.
• This development is crucial as neglected tropical diseases disproportionately affect impoverished populations in India and globally, often lacking adequate research funding.
• The vaccine utilizes a novel platform technology, potentially allowing for rapid scaling up of production once approved.
• This achievement underscores India's growing capabilities in biotechnology and its commitment to addressing public health challenges through indigenous solutions.
gsSubTopics: Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
articles.source: National Institute of Immunology / The Economic Times
prelimsPoints: Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) are a diverse group of communicable diseases that prevail in tropical and subtropical conditions.
Examples of NTDs include Dengue, Chikungunya, Kala-azar, Lymphatic Filariasis, Leprosy.
National Institute of Immunology (NII) is a premier research institution in India.
Vaccine development involves several stages: exploratory, preclinical, clinical trials (Phase I, II, III), regulatory review, and manufacturing.
India is a major global producer of vaccines.
mainsPoints: Significance of indigenous vaccine development for neglected tropical diseases in addressing health disparities and achieving health equity.
Challenges in research and development for NTDs, including lack of funding, market incentives, and complex disease biology.
Role of public and private sector collaboration in accelerating vaccine development and ensuring accessibility.
Impact of such breakthroughs on India's public health infrastructure and its global leadership in vaccine manufacturing.
Ethical considerations and regulatory frameworks for rapid development and deployment of new vaccines.
articles.imageDescription: Scientists in a lab examining samples under a microscope, symbolizing medical research.
articles.trivia: India achieved significant success in eliminating Yaws, an NTD, in 2016 and is working towards eliminating several others.

articles.headline: National Disaster Management Authority Releases New Guidelines for Urban Flood Management
articles.explainer:
• The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) released a comprehensive set of new guidelines for Urban Flood Management on January 22, 2025.
• The guidelines emphasize a multi-pronged approach, including real-time forecasting, improved drainage infrastructure, and community-based preparedness.
• They advocate for the integration of climate resilience and nature-based solutions into urban planning to mitigate flood risks.
• States and Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are mandated to develop specific action plans based on these guidelines, incorporating early warning systems and emergency response protocols.
• The move comes in response to increasing instances of urban flooding exacerbated by climate change and unplanned urbanization.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation.
prelimsPoints: NDMA is the apex body for disaster management in India.
It functions under the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Urban flooding is caused by a combination of meteorological, hydrological, and anthropogenic factors.
Early warning systems are crucial for disaster preparedness.
National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) is the specialized force for disaster response.
mainsPoints: Causes and consequences of urban flooding in India, including the role of climate change and unplanned urbanization.
Effectiveness of current disaster management frameworks in addressing urban specific vulnerabilities.
Importance of integrated urban planning, incorporating green infrastructure and water sensitive design, for flood mitigation.
Challenges in implementation of guidelines by Urban Local Bodies (ULBs), including funding, capacity, and inter-agency coordination.
Role of community participation and public awareness in building urban resilience against floods.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a flooded urban area with rescue boats, emphasizing the impact of urban floods.
articles.trivia: The Disaster Management Act was enacted in 2005, leading to the establishment of NDMA.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Temple Remains in Karnataka
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced on January 22, 2025, the discovery of significant ancient temple remains during excavations in the historical region of North Karnataka.
• The findings include remnants of a large temple complex, intricate carvings, and pottery dating back to the Chalukyan and Rashtrakuta periods.
• The discovery sheds new light on the architectural styles and religious practices prevalent in the Deccan plateau during early medieval times.
• Conservation efforts are underway to preserve the site, and further excavations are planned to uncover the full extent of the complex.
• This archaeological find is expected to contribute valuable insights into India's rich cultural heritage and historical narratives.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
articles.source: Archaeological Survey of India / Deccan Herald
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
The Chalukyas (Badami Chalukyas) ruled parts of South and Central India from the 6th to 12th centuries.
The Rashtrakutas succeeded the Badami Chalukyas and ruled from the 8th to 10th centuries.
Karnataka is known for its rich architectural heritage, including sites like Hampi, Aihole, Badami, and Pattadakal.
Pattadakal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site known for its Chalukyan architecture.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists carefully excavating ancient ruins, revealing carved stone pillars.
articles.trivia: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora Caves, a UNESCO World Heritage site, was carved out of a single rock by Rashtrakuta king Krishna I.

articles.headline: Government Enhances Public Grievance Redressal System with AI-Powered Integration
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Administrative Reforms and Public Grievances (DARPG) announced on January 22, 2025, the integration of advanced AI tools into the Centralized Public Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (CPGRAMS).
• The AI-powered system aims to expedite grievance resolution by intelligently categorizing complaints, routing them to the appropriate authorities, and identifying systemic issues.
• This enhancement is expected to significantly improve the efficiency, transparency, and accountability of public service delivery.
• Citizens will benefit from faster response times and more effective redressal mechanisms, fostering trust in government services.
• The initiative aligns with the government's vision of 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' and leveraging technology for citizen-centric administration.
gsSubTopics: Governance, Transparency and Accountability, e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
keyTerms: Centralized Public Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (CPGRAMS)
prelimsPoints: CPGRAMS is an online platform for citizens to lodge grievances with central and state government organizations.
DARPG (Department of Administrative Reforms and Public Grievances) is the nodal agency for CPGRAMS.
AI applications in governance include data analysis, predictive modeling, and automated response systems.
The concept of 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' emphasizes efficient and effective governance.
The Right to Public Services Act aims to ensure timely delivery of services to citizens.
mainsPoints: Significance of public grievance redressal mechanisms in promoting good governance and citizen trust.
Potential benefits of AI integration in improving efficiency, transparency, and accountability of administrative processes.
Challenges associated with AI adoption in governance, including data privacy, algorithmic bias, and digital literacy.
Impact on administrative reforms and the shift towards citizen-centric service delivery.
Measures needed to ensure ethical use of AI, build public confidence, and address the digital divide in accessing such services.
articles.imageDescription: A graphical representation of CPGRAMS portal interface with an AI icon, symbolizing smart governance.
articles.trivia: CPGRAMS receives millions of grievances annually, covering a wide range of public services and departments.

articles.headline: India Hosts G20 Follow-up Meet on Sustainable Development Goals Implementation
articles.explainer:
• India, as part of its ongoing engagement with the G20, hosted a high-level follow-up meeting on January 22, 2025, focusing on accelerating the implementation of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
• The meeting brought together representatives from G20 nations, international organizations, and civil society to discuss progress and challenges in achieving the 2030 Agenda.
• Key discussions revolved around financing for development, technological cooperation, and strengthening multilateralism to address global inequalities.
• India presented its national strategies for SDG localization and emphasized the need for a holistic approach to sustainable development.
• The outcome highlighted the importance of collective action and renewed political commitment to rescue the SDGs amidst global crises.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
articles.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Economic Times
prelimsPoints: G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
The 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) were adopted by all UN member states in 2015.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
SDGs cover social, economic, and environmental development issues.
The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development is a blueprint for peace and prosperity for people and the planet.
mainsPoints: Significance of G20 as a platform for global economic governance and its role in achieving the SDGs.
Challenges in SDG implementation, including insufficient financing, geopolitical tensions, and climate change impacts.
India's leadership and initiatives in promoting sustainable development and SDG localization.
Importance of international cooperation and multilateralism in addressing complex global challenges.
Pathways for accelerating SDG progress, focusing on equitable development, digital transformation, and green transitions.
articles.imageDescription: A G20 logo superimposed on a map of the world, with SDG icons around it.
articles.trivia: The G20 represents around 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

articles.headline: Indigenous Aircraft Carrier Vikrant Completes Final Phase of Airfield Compatibility Trials
articles.explainer:
• INS Vikrant, India's first indigenously built aircraft carrier, successfully completed its final phase of airfield compatibility trials (AFT) on January 22, 2025.
• The trials involved extensive operations of various fixed-wing aircraft, including MiG-29K fighter jets and naval utility helicopters, from its deck.
• This milestone signifies the carrier's full operational readiness to handle a diverse range of aircraft, enhancing India's naval power projection capabilities.
• The successful completion of these trials underscores India's growing self-reliance in defense manufacturing and complex naval engineering.
• INS Vikrant is expected to be fully integrated into the Western Naval Command, significantly boosting India's maritime security posture in the Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: INS Vikrant is India's first indigenously designed and built aircraft carrier.
It was commissioned into the Indian Navy in September 2022.
Built by Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL).
It operates the STOBAR (Short Take-Off But Arrested Recovery) system for aircraft launch and recovery.
MiG-29K is a Russian-origin carrier-borne multirole fighter aircraft.
mainsPoints: Significance of indigenous aircraft carriers for India's naval power projection and maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Contribution of 'Make in India' initiative to defense indigenization and technological self-reliance.
Challenges and complexities involved in designing, building, and operationalizing an aircraft carrier.
Impact on India's strategic autonomy and its ability to respond to emerging geopolitical challenges in the maritime domain.
Future prospects for India's naval modernization program and the development of more indigenous defense platforms.
articles.imageDescription: INS Vikrant sailing in the open sea, with fighter jets on its deck.
articles.trivia: INS Vikrant is named after India's first aircraft carrier, which was acquired from the UK in 1961.

articles.headline: Ministry of Education Releases Progress Report on National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 Implementation
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released its annual progress report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 on January 22, 2025.
• The report highlighted significant strides in curricular reforms, teacher training, and integrating technology into education across various states.
• Key achievements include the development of new National Curricular Frameworks (NCFs) and the establishment of multidisciplinary higher education institutions.
• Challenges remain in ensuring equitable access, particularly for marginalized sections, and addressing infrastructure gaps in remote areas.
• The Ministry reiterated its commitment to the NEP's transformative vision, aiming to make India a global knowledge superpower.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
articles.source: Ministry of Education / Press Information Bureau
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
Aims to achieve 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.
Emphasizes multidisciplinary education, critical thinking, and vocational training.
National Research Foundation (NRF) is proposed to foster research culture.
mainsPoints: Transformative potential of NEP 2020 in reforming India's education system to meet 21st-century demands.
Challenges in effective implementation of NEP, including funding, teacher training, infrastructure, and overcoming resistance to change.
Impact of curricular reforms and multidisciplinary approach on student learning outcomes and skill development.
Role of technology and digital education in achieving NEP goals, particularly for equitable access.
Measures needed to address disparities in educational access and quality, especially for disadvantaged groups.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, engaged in learning with digital tools.
articles.trivia: NEP 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New Foreign Trade Policy Aimed at Boosting Exports and Global Competitiveness
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Commerce and Industry unveiled India's new Foreign Trade Policy (FTP) 2025-30 on January 22, 2025, with a strong focus on enhancing exports.
• The policy introduces streamlined procedures, reduced compliance burden, and incentives for e-commerce exports and green products.
• It aims to achieve an ambitious export target of $2 trillion by 2030, positioning India as a major player in global trade.
• Special emphasis is placed on supporting MSMEs, promoting district-as-export-hubs initiatives, and diversifying export markets.
• The FTP is designed to align with India's 'Make in India' and 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiatives, fostering a competitive and resilient export ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Investment models.
articles.source: Ministry of Commerce and Industry / Livemint
prelimsPoints: Foreign Trade Policy is announced by the Ministry of Commerce and Industry.
It provides a framework for boosting exports and imports.
SEZs (Special Economic Zones) are designated areas with special economic regulations to promote exports.
MSMEs contribute significantly to India's exports and employment.
The policy aims to simplify procedures and reduce transaction costs.
mainsPoints: Significance of a robust Foreign Trade Policy in achieving India's economic growth and global trade ambitions.
Challenges in boosting exports, including global economic slowdown, protectionist policies, and infrastructure bottlenecks.
Impact of policy incentives on MSMEs and their integration into global value chains.
Role of e-commerce and digital platforms in diversifying India's export basket and reaching new markets.
Measures needed to enhance India's competitiveness in manufacturing and services sectors to achieve export targets.
articles.imageDescription: A container ship at a bustling port, symbolizing international trade and exports.
articles.trivia: India's merchandise and services exports crossed $770 billion in FY23, a record high.

highlights: India launches second phase of National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting 5 MMT annual production by 2030, aiming for global leadership.
Supreme Court reaffirms the independent and autonomous powers of State Election Commissions in conducting local body polls.
India-France Strategic Dialogue concludes, deepening cooperation in Indo-Pacific security, defense, and critical technologies.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims to achieve 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of annual green hydrogen production capacity by 2030.
2. The Ministry of Power is the nodal ministry for the implementation of NGHM.
3. The SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a key component of the mission.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The NGHM aims for 5 MMT annual green hydrogen production by 2030. Statement 2 is incorrect. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for NGHM. Statement 3 is correct. SIGHT programme is indeed a key component of the mission, providing incentives for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution deal with the powers and functions of the State Election Commission for conducting elections to Panchayats and Municipalities?
(a) Articles 324 and 325
(b) Articles 243K and 243ZA
(c) Articles 243G and 243W
(d) Articles 280 and 281
prelimsMocks.explanation: Articles 243K (for Panchayats) and 243ZA (for Municipalities) specifically provide for the constitution and powers of the State Election Commission for local body elections. Articles 324 and 325 deal with the Election Commission of India for parliamentary and state legislative elections. Articles 243G and 243W deal with powers, authority, and responsibilities of Panchayats and Municipalities respectively. Articles 280 and 281 deal with the Finance Commission.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV):
1. PSLV is ISRO's third-generation launch vehicle.
2. It is primarily used for launching geostationary satellites.
3. India's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) was launched using PSLV.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; PSLV is indeed ISRO's third-generation launch vehicle. Statement 2 is incorrect; PSLV is primarily known for launching Earth Observation/Remote Sensing satellites into sun-synchronous polar orbits, while GSLV is used for geostationary satellites. Statement 3 is correct; the Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) was successfully launched by PSLV-C25.

prelimsMocks.question:
The Montreux Record is a register maintained under which of the following international conventions?
(a) Convention on Biological Diversity
(b) Ramsar Convention
(c) Convention on Migratory Species
(d) UNESCO World Heritage Convention
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Montreux Record is a register of Ramsar Sites where changes in ecological character have occurred, are occurring, or are likely to occur as a result of technological developments, pollution, or other human interference. It is maintained as part of the Ramsar List of Wetlands of International Importance.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) is/are correct?
1. It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
2. The Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) ID is a mandatory requirement for all citizens to access healthcare services.
3. The National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; ABDM aims to build a national digital health ecosystem. Statement 2 is incorrect; the ABHA ID is a voluntary opt-in service, not a mandatory requirement. Statement 3 is correct; the National Health Authority (NHA) is indeed the implementing agency for ABDM.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the strategic significance of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission in achieving energy security and climate targets. What are the key challenges in its implementation, and how can they be addressed? (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Energy, Infrastructure, Environmental pollution & degradation, Science & Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NGHM and its objectives. Elaborate on strategic significance covering energy security (reduced import dependence), climate targets (decarbonization, NDC goals), and economic benefits (job creation, export potential). Discuss challenges like high cost, technology gap, infrastructure, and water availability. Suggest solutions including PLI schemes, R&D investment, international collaboration, and policy support.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's reaffirmation of the State Election Commissions' autonomy is crucial for strengthening democratic decentralization. Critically analyze the challenges faced by SECs in India and suggest measures to enhance their independence and effectiveness. (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein. Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with the context of the Supreme Court ruling and the constitutional basis of SECs (Articles 243K/ZA). Discuss challenges: political interference, lack of financial autonomy, delayed appointment/removal, limited staff, non-cooperation from state governments. Suggest measures: constitutional amendments for stronger safeguards, independent budget, fixed tenure/security of office, clear separation of powers, empowering SECs with contempt powers, greater public awareness.

mainsMocks.question: India's growing capabilities in space technology are vital for national development and strategic autonomy. Examine the applications of Earth Observation Satellites in India and highlight the opportunities and challenges for the Indian space program in the coming decade. (15 marks)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology. Awareness in the fields of Space.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly introduce India's space program and its focus on indigenous development. Detail applications of Earth Observation Satellites: agriculture (crop forecasting, soil health), disaster management (flood mapping, cyclone tracking), urban planning, resource management, climate monitoring, defense. Discuss opportunities: private sector participation, global commercial launch market, deep space missions, advanced satellite technologies. Address challenges: funding, technological gaps, international competition, space debris management, human resource development.

GS1.headline: Renowned Urdu Poet and Scholar Tabish Mehdi Passes Away at 73
GS1.explainer:
• Celebrated Urdu poet, critic, and scholar, Tabish Mehdi, passed away at the age of 73 on January 22, 2025.
• Mehdi was known for his profound contribution to modern Urdu poetry, combining classical aesthetics with contemporary themes.
• His works often explored themes of humanism, social justice, and philosophical introspection, resonating with a wide audience.
• He was also a respected academic, having taught at various universities and contributing significantly to Urdu literary criticism.
• His demise marks a significant loss for the Urdu literary world, leaving behind a rich legacy of poetry and critical thought.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: Tabish Mehdi was a prominent figure in modern Urdu poetry.
Known for blending classical and contemporary styles.
His work often reflected themes of social consciousness and philosophical depth.
Urdu is one of the 22 scheduled languages of India.
Urdu literature has a rich history in India, with notable poets like Ghalib, Iqbal, and Faiz.
GS1.imageDescription: A black and white portrait of Tabish Mehdi, deep in thought.
GS1.trivia: Urdu developed in the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal Empire, with significant influences from Persian, Arabic, and local Indo-Aryan languages.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Temple Remains in Karnataka
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced on January 22, 2025, the discovery of significant ancient temple remains during excavations in the historical region of North Karnataka.
• The findings include remnants of a large temple complex, intricate carvings, and pottery dating back to the Chalukyan and Rashtrakuta periods.
• The discovery sheds new light on the architectural styles and religious practices prevalent in the Deccan plateau during early medieval times.
• Conservation efforts are underway to preserve the site, and further excavations are planned to uncover the full extent of the complex.
• This archaeological find is expected to contribute valuable insights into India's rich cultural heritage and historical narratives.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
GS1.source: Archaeological Survey of India / Deccan Herald
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
The Chalukyas (Badami Chalukyas) ruled parts of South and Central India from the 6th to 12th centuries.
The Rashtrakutas succeeded the Badami Chalukyas and ruled from the 8th to 10th centuries.
Karnataka is known for its rich architectural heritage, including sites like Hampi, Aihole, Badami, and Pattadakal.
Pattadakal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site known for its Chalukyan architecture.
GS1.imageDescription: Archaeologists carefully excavating ancient ruins, revealing carved stone pillars.
GS1.trivia: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora Caves, a UNESCO World Heritage site, was carved out of a single rock by Rashtrakuta king Krishna I.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Autonomy of State Election Commissions in Local Body Polls
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, on January 22, 2025, reaffirmed the independent and autonomous nature of State Election Commissions (SECs) in conducting local body elections.
• The ruling clarified that state governments cannot unduly interfere with the schedule or conduct of Panchayat and Municipal elections.
• The court emphasized the constitutional mandate of SECs under Articles 243K and 243ZA, highlighting their role in ensuring free and fair elections at the grassroots level.
• This judgment reinforces the federal structure and the principles of democratic decentralization enshrined in the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments.
• It called for greater cooperation between state governments and SECs to uphold the integrity of the electoral process.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: State Election Commissions are constitutional bodies responsible for conducting elections to Panchayats and Municipalities.
Articles 243K (Panchayats) and 243ZA (Municipalities) deal with SECs.
The conditions of service and tenure of an SEC cannot be varied to his disadvantage after his appointment.
SEC enjoys the same status as a judge of a High Court.
The 73rd and 74th Amendment Acts gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions and Urban Local Bodies, respectively.
mainsPoints: Significance of SEC's autonomy for ensuring free and fair elections at the grassroots level, strengthening democratic decentralization.
Challenges posed by state interference in SEC's functions and its impact on the credibility of local governance.
Role of judicial intervention in upholding constitutional provisions and safeguarding the independence of statutory bodies.
Implications for federal relations and the balance of power between state governments and autonomous electoral bodies.
Need for electoral reforms at the local level to enhance transparency and accountability.
GS2.imageDescription: Scales of justice with a map of India in the background, symbolizing judicial oversight on federal structures.
GS2.trivia: The first State Election Commission was established in Madhya Pradesh in 1994.

GS2.headline: India-France Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizing Deeper Indo-Pacific Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue, held in New Delhi on January 22, 2025, concluded with a joint statement reinforcing commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, counter-terrorism efforts, space technology collaboration, and maritime security.
• Both nations reiterated their resolve to address regional and global challenges, including climate change and cyber security threats.
• India welcomed France's continued engagement in the Indo-Pacific, recognizing its role as a resident power with significant interests.
• New initiatives were announced for joint research and development in critical and emerging technologies, solidifying the strategic partnership.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
GS2.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Times of India
prelimsPoints: India and France established a Strategic Partnership in 1998.
France is a key partner for India in defense, nuclear energy, and space.
India's Rafale fighter jets are sourced from France.
Both nations are committed to multilateralism and rules-based international order.
France has significant overseas territories in the Indo-Pacific (e.g., Reunion, New Caledonia).
mainsPoints: Significance of India-France strategic partnership in promoting stability and security in the Indo-Pacific region.
How bilateral cooperation in defense and space contributes to India's strategic autonomy and technological self-reliance.
Role of India-France collaboration in addressing global challenges like climate change, terrorism, and cyber security.
Impact of France's Indo-Pacific strategy on regional dynamics and its alignment with India's Act East Policy.
Challenges and opportunities in expanding economic and cultural ties between the two nations.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and France displayed during a joint press conference.
GS2.trivia: France was the first P5 country to support India's bid for a permanent seat in the UN Security Council.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Reach to Remote and Tribal Areas
GS2.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced the expansion of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) to cover remote and tribal regions across India on January 22, 2025.
• This expansion aims to bridge the digital health divide and ensure equitable access to digital health services for underserved populations.
• Special focus will be given to creating ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) IDs and digitizing health records in these areas through mobile health units and community health workers.
• The initiative includes training local healthcare providers in using digital tools and improving telemedicine infrastructure.
• This move is crucial for achieving universal health coverage and integrating these populations into the national digital health ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched in September 2021 by the Prime Minister.
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
It is a voluntary opt-in scheme for citizens.
mainsPoints: Significance of digital health initiatives in improving healthcare access and quality, especially in remote areas.
Challenges in implementing digital health solutions in tribal and underserved regions (digital literacy, connectivity, infrastructure).

Ethical considerations around data privacy and security in a national digital health ecosystem.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a smartphone with health app icons, connecting remote areas to healthcare.
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) provides health insurance coverage to over 50 crore beneficiaries.

GS2.headline: India Advocates for Enhanced Climate Finance Commitments at UN Negotiations
GS2.explainer:
• India's delegation actively participated in the ongoing UN climate finance negotiations on January 22, 2025, advocating for developed nations to fulfill their commitments.
• India emphasized the need for a clear roadmap and increased financial flows from developed to developing countries for climate mitigation and adaptation.
• The discussions focused on operationalizing the new collective quantified goal (NCQG) on climate finance post-2025, moving beyond the unfulfilled $100 billion target.
• India highlighted the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC) in determining finance contributions.
• The talks are crucial in setting the stage for future climate action and ensuring equity in global climate efforts.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
GS2.source: Ministry of External Affairs / The Times of India

prelimsPoints: UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change) is the primary international treaty on climate change.
Developed countries pledged $100 billion per year in climate finance by 2020, which has not been fully met.
NCQG (New Collective Quantified Goal) is being negotiated for climate finance post-2025.
CBDR-RC is a principle under UNFCCC recognizing different responsibilities based on historical emissions and capabilities.
India is a key voice for developing countries in climate negotiations.
mainsPoints: Importance of climate finance for developing countries to meet their climate goals and adapt to climate change impacts.
Challenges in mobilizing adequate and predictable climate finance, including issues of definition, transparency, and accessibility.
India's stance on climate justice, equity, and the principle of CBDR-RC in global climate negotiations.
Role of international cooperation and multilateral institutions in facilitating climate finance and technology transfer.
Implications of climate finance outcomes for India's energy transition, sustainable development, and global standing.
GS2.imageDescription: Delegates from various countries seated around a table during a UN climate negotiation.
GS2.trivia: The Green Climate Fund (GCF) is a fund established within the framework of the UNFCCC to assist developing countries in adaptation and mitigation practices.

GS2.headline: Government Enhances Public Grievance Redressal System with AI-Powered Integration
GS2.explainer:
• The Department of Administrative Reforms and Public Grievances (DARPG) announced on January 22, 2025, the integration of advanced AI tools into the Centralized Public Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (CPGRAMS).
• The AI-powered system aims to expedite grievance resolution by intelligently categorizing complaints, routing them to the appropriate authorities, and identifying systemic issues.
• This enhancement is expected to significantly improve the efficiency, transparency, and accountability of public service delivery.
• Citizens will benefit from faster response times and more effective redressal mechanisms, fostering trust in government services.
• The initiative aligns with the government's vision of 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' and leveraging technology for citizen-centric administration.
gsSubTopics: Governance, Transparency and Accountability, e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.

prelimsPoints: CPGRAMS is an online platform for citizens to lodge grievances with central and state government organizations.

AI applications in governance include data analysis, predictive modeling, and automated response systems.
The concept of 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' emphasizes efficient and effective governance.
The Right to Public Services Act aims to ensure timely delivery of services to citizens.
mainsPoints: Significance of public grievance redressal mechanisms in promoting good governance and citizen trust.

GS2.trivia: CPGRAMS receives millions of grievances annually, covering a wide range of public services and departments.

GS2.headline: India Hosts G20 Follow-up Meet on Sustainable Development Goals Implementation
GS2.explainer:
• India, as part of its ongoing engagement with the G20, hosted a high-level follow-up meeting on January 22, 2025, focusing on accelerating the implementation of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
• The meeting brought together representatives from G20 nations, international organizations, and civil society to discuss progress and challenges in achieving the 2030 Agenda.
• Key discussions revolved around financing for development, technological cooperation, and strengthening multilateralism to address global inequalities.
• India presented its national strategies for SDG localization and emphasized the need for a holistic approach to sustainable development.
• The outcome highlighted the importance of collective action and renewed political commitment to rescue the SDGs amidst global crises.

GS2.explainer:

• The report highlighted significant strides in curricular reforms, teacher training, and integrating technology into education across various states.
• Key achievements include the development of new National Curricular Frameworks (NCFs) and the establishment of multidisciplinary higher education institutions.
• Challenges remain in ensuring equitable access, particularly for marginalized sections, and addressing infrastructure gaps in remote areas.

GS3.headline: India Unveils Second Phase of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Targeting Global Leadership
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) announced the second phase of the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) on January 22, 2025.
• This phase focuses on scaling up production capabilities, establishing dedicated green hydrogen corridors, and boosting domestic manufacturing of electrolysers.
• Key targets include achieving 5 million tonnes of annual green hydrogen production capacity by 2030 and significant reduction in green hydrogen production costs.
• The mission aims to position India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization goals.
• New incentives for R&D in fuel cell technology and green hydrogen applications in hard-to-abate sectors like steel and heavy-duty transport are part of the expanded scope.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure
Environmental pollution & degradation
Science & Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Mobilization of resources

SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a key component.

mainsPoints: Economic Impact: Job creation, investment attraction, reduced fossil fuel imports (energy security), export potential, industrial growth.
